meta_notes: Both feeds rebuilt clean this morning (261 signal videos, 38 stitch candidates). Underlying Apify extract is 2026-07-19T09:15Z — ~21h old, so ages skew ~1d conservative; no fallback was needed. News shelf is deliberately EMPTY: every candidate failed the ≤7d story-date test — bemotrizinol is the June 9 story (the July 14 WBUR piece is local coverage of it, and its Aug 9 effective date is display-only, per the standing rule), the Banana Boat benzene recall dates to July 2022 with a January 2023 expansion, and the Snopes sunscreen/skin-cancer fact-check covers a claim that circulated in late June. Trade-press acne and psoriasis pipeline items were undated roundups. Nothing padded. Virality sweep: 10 candidates cleared ≥1M views or ≥200K/day — 4 in the shortlist, 6 in more_stitches (cap extended from 4 per the guarantee rule), 2 of those as unidentified stubs. Tracked derm accounts above 1M (@dr.derm.love, @dermguru, @dermatologysurgeon, @dr.zionko, @jillian.gottlieb) are excluded from stitching by the builder and surface as theme receipts instead.

more_topics.note: The single biggest ♥ magnet on the board. “Comment BARRIER CREAM and I'll send you…” — a community prompt, exactly the follow-generating profile. Steal the mechanic, not the topic.
Collab format — four derms, one reel. She is already inside this circle; ask to be in the next one.
Biggest raw wave of the day but it is foodborne GI, not derm. Steal the 7s public-health-alert cadence; skip the topic.
Small-creator 3% + gold. Isotretinoin series content over-indexes on follows — a multi-part Accutane series is the magnet-profile play.
The quiz card grown up — guess in the comments, then a 61s pathology reveal. Comment-bait plus substance.
Thin on breakout but it is the hair/scalp lane she under-exploits — thicker beard, brows and lashes as the ‘side effect’ hook.

her_wins.summary: (IG/TT) Syphilis rash went superviral overnight — 441K (IG, 5% breakout) + 264K (TT) + 20K (YT) = ~725K in 1.6d. Her fastest-moving post on the board at 11,991 views/hr.
her_wins.open_lane: part 2 — the palms-and-soles rash is the tell almost nobody knows; make that its own card before the wave cools

her_wins.summary: (TT/IG) ♥ FOLLOWER MAGNET — pricey skin & hair picks: 685K (TT, ♥ silver, top 8% engagement) + 328K (IG) = ~1.01M in 4.6d. Followers are working here, not just reach.
her_wins.open_lane: the SAVE half — same format, drugstore dupes; extend the series rather than pivot, the magnet profile favours series

her_wins.summary: (TT/IG) ♥ GOLD MAGNET still running — glass-skin routine: 1.60M (TT, superviral, 5%, ♥ gold, top 1% engagement) + 1.33M (IG, ♥ silver) = ~2.93M in 11.7d. Her strongest follower generator of the month.
her_wins.open_lane: the pregnancy-safe glass-skin version — she flagged ‘don't use a retinoid when pregnant’ in the caption and never made the follow-up

stitch_shortlist.author: @itvnews
stitch_shortlist.claim: children as young as four are using adult anti-ageing skincare; dermatologists want warning labels

rubric.misses: None — clean 6/6. A news package, so the stitch is you answering the reporter's open question.

pitch.the_claim: Four-year-olds are using retinol and acid serums made for adults, and dermatologists are asking regulators for warning labels.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 1.60M in 2.8d at 573K/day with 4,136 comments — the heaviest comment section of any candidate today, and the argument in there is parents vs parents. A national broadcaster asked the question; a Harvard dermatologist answering it on camera is the obvious missing shot.
pitch.your_fit: Directly corpus-grounded: her teen-skincare guide already sets the age line, and her retinoid ladder gives her the specific 'what a kid's skin actually does with tretinoin' answer. She has never stitched a news package — new format, zero risk.
angles: empathy-first: your kid isn't the problem, the marketing is
‘you want me to WHAT?’ — retinol on a four-year-old
switch-don't-quit: the three-product routine a kid actually needs
pitch.length: original eats ~8–10s; 35–60s total — news stitches need room to answer

stitch_shortlist.author: @juliaburdine
stitch_shortlist.claim: slathering zinc-oxide diaper rash cream on your face overnight gives you glass skin

rubric.misses: None — 6/6. Heat scores 1 on raw size but it is pacing 213K views/day and it is the civilian end of a wave @dermguru just rode to 3.50M.

pitch.the_claim: Diaper rash cream as an overnight face mask — the 'zinc oxide does everything' hack, pitched as a glass-skin shortcut.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 724K in 3.4d and still rising, and it sits underneath the day's biggest ♥ gold magnet: @dermguru's 3.50M barrier reel opens by name-checking diaper paste. The hack is being litigated on the FYP right now by @labmuffinbeautyscience and @ciaransmythe — the dermatologist voice is missing from that argument.
pitch.your_fit: Her strongest possible ground. She already has a 2.4M-view video whose hook is verbatim: 'Dermatologist here — and a zinc oxide based diaper cream is not the best for repairing a damaged skin barrier.' That was about barriers; this wave is about GLASS SKIN and SPF — a genuinely new angle on a claim she owns.
angles: she already called this — same cream, new bad idea
switch-don't-quit: keep the zinc, use the tested one
empathy-first: it 'works' for one night, here's why
pitch.length: original eats ~6s; 30–45s total — the long explainer is what's winning this wave

stitch_shortlist.author: @kbeauty_code
stitch_shortlist.claim: what a Korean dermatologist and pharmacist actually use on their own skin

rubric.misses: Punch direction is delicate — these are real clinicians, so the only honest frame is 'yes, and here's the US equivalent'.

pitch.the_claim: A Korean dermatologist and pharmacist name the ingredients they personally use — framed as insider access US viewers can't get.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 940K in 2.8d at 336K/day. It rides the same 'what do the pros actually buy' energy as the worth-the-money wave she is already winning in, and it hands her a natural counterpart: the American derm's version.
pitch.your_fit: Corpus-grounded and unusually well-timed — her US-vs-European/Asian sunscreen explainer is 1.0d old and makes exactly this regulatory point: the FDA has strictly controlled which filters get approved. She can answer 'why can't I buy that here' with authority.
angles: ‘yes, and’ — here's the US shelf equivalent of each
why the FDA won't let you buy half of these
the two she'd actually keep, the two that are hype

stitch_shortlist.author: @the.art.of
stitch_shortlist.claim: chemical SPF 50 is the hill to die on — it covers UVA, not just the burn rays

rubric.misses: Miss on #4 — 6.7d old, past the rising window. Kept because it is 1.03M with 1,071 comments and it is a civilian defending sunscreen while the anti-SPF wave runs, which is rare and worth amplifying.

pitch.the_claim: A civilian goes to bat for chemical SPF 50 — UVB burns, UVA ages, and she is done apologising for wearing it.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 1.03M with 1,071 comments and the comment section is the whole anti-sunscreen argument in miniature. Rubric #6 says don't punch down at people being correct — so this is the 'yes, and' stitch: she is right, and a dermatologist saying so out loud is worth more than another debunk.
pitch.your_fit: Squarely corpus-grounded. Her own post six days ago says it in her words: 'Sunscreen doesn't cause cancer. The myth that sunscreen causes cancer evolves from evidence that some chemical filters are absorbed…' She has the receipts to back this creator up.
angles: ‘yes, and’ — she's right, here's the study behind it
UVA is the aging one — make that the whole point
the absorbed-filter myth, answered in 20 seconds

stitch_shortlist.author: @ruby_sb_
stitch_shortlist.claim: how to cover a bald spot from scalp psoriasis with a headscarf

rubric.misses: Miss on #5 — 227K is modest. Kept deliberately: it is the scalp/hair lane she under-exploits, it is 2.0d old and climbing at 114K/day, and her companion post is at 242K with 252 comments.

pitch.the_claim: A creator with scalp psoriasis and Crohn's shows how she styles around the bald spot — coping, not treating.
pitch.why_it_stitches: Rising fast (114K/day at 2.0d) and the comments are full of people asking whether the hair grows back. Nobody in that thread is a dermatologist. This is the empathy-first react she does well, and scalp is her highest-ceiling under-served area.
pitch.your_fit: Needs her take on scalp psoriasis specifically — the corpus has folliculitis and general scalp content but no scalp-psoriasis regrowth position. Do not claim a prognosis on camera without checking it first.
angles: empathy-first: the scarf is fine — and it's treatable
scalp psoriasis vs seborrheic dermatitis, told apart
does the hair come back? answer it plainly

stitch_shortlist.author: @labmuffinbeautyscience
stitch_shortlist.claim: the viral DIY test showing diaper cream gives 100% UV protection is an invalid test

rubric.misses: Misses #4 and #5 — 4.9d old and only 57K. Included because the claim is the sharpest in the whole zinc wave and the comments (519 on 57K views) are running hot; the value is the argument, not the audience.

pitch.the_claim: Zinc oxide diaper cream performs poorly in real SPF testing, so a DIY 'test' showing a thin layer blocking 100% of UV proves nothing.
pitch.why_it_stitches: A cosmetic chemist made the technical case; the missing half is the clinical one — what actually walks into a Mohs clinic after a summer of believing this. Comment-to-view ratio is roughly 9x normal, which is the controversy signal.
pitch.your_fit: Grounded and complementary. Her zinc-oxide-diaper-cream position is already on record at 2.4M views. Rubric #6 applies — this is a fellow expert being correct, so the frame is 'yes, and here's what I see downstream', never a correction.
angles: ‘yes, and’ — the chemistry checks out, here's the clinic
why a UV camera is not an SPF test
what 40% zinc actually buys you on a face

more_stitches.author: @radiantskinclinics
more_stitches.claim: an azelaic acid compound cream clears melasma (Nepali-language clinic promotion)

rubric.misses: Misses #4 and #6 — 5.2d old, and it is a clinic advertising its own compounded cream, so a stitch risks punching down at a small overseas practice. Surfaced because 2.32M at 447K/day clears the guarantee.

pitch.the_claim: A compounded azelaic acid cream is presented as a melasma fix.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 2.32M in 5.2d — the second-largest in-lane wave on the board, and azelaic acid is one of her named seed topics. The doubt to state plainly: it is a clinic ad in Nepali, so watch it before deciding whether the target is the claim or the marketing.
pitch.your_fit: Azelaic acid is well covered in her corpus; the melasma-specific efficacy claim needs her take before she says anything on camera.
angles: what azelaic acid actually does for melasma
why melasma comes back no matter the cream

more_stitches.author: @chiegeskincare
more_stitches.claim: a creator shares a severe ongoing facial skin condition with no diagnosis named

rubric.misses: Misses #1, #4 and #6 — no claim to answer, 7.0d old, and stitching a person in visible distress punches down. Surfaced only because 2.29M at 327K/day clears the guarantee.

pitch.the_claim: A creator in visible distress about a worsening facial skin condition; 6,219 comments are guessing at diagnoses.
pitch.why_it_stitches: It does not, as a stitch. But it is the third-biggest in-lane wave today and the comment section is a crowd self-diagnosing a stranger — that is the story, not the person.
pitch.your_fit: If she touches it, cover the CONDITION as a standalone, never the creator. Do not diagnose from video.
angles: why you can't diagnose a rash from a video
when a skin condition means see someone this week

more_stitches.author: @hevadametto
more_stitches.claim: an 80-year-old's skin as proof of a skincare approach (Portuguese-language POV)

rubric.misses: Miss on lane certainty — the caption ('80 years old?') implies an ageless-skin claim but the actual argument is unreadable from a 6s Portuguese POV. Watch before pitching.

pitch.the_claim: An 80-year-old's skin presented as the payoff of some routine or habit — the specific claim is not legible from the caption.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 2.16M in 3.6d at 600K/day, the single fastest in-lane wave today, and 'she's 80?!' reveals are reliable react bait. Doubt stated: it may be genetics-and-sun-avoidance rather than any product.
pitch.your_fit: Her anti-aging long-form guide covers the honest version — what actually ages skin, in what proportion. Strong ground once the claim is confirmed.
angles: what 80-year-old skin actually tells you
sun avoidance beats every serum on that shelf

more_stitches.author: @febisola_iris
more_stitches.claim: a two-product cream-and-sun-water combo delivers hydrated, protected skin

rubric.misses: Misses #4 and lane purity — 4.4d old and it is a brand plug inside lifestyle content. Surfaced because 1.25M clears the guarantee; the doubt is stated rather than used to drop it.

pitch.the_claim: A 'sun water' toner plus a sorbet cream is framed as sun protection.
pitch.why_it_stitches: 1.25M in 4.4d, and the interesting part is the vocabulary: 'sun water' reads as sun protection to a viewer and is not sunscreen. That mislabelling is squarely her fight this week.
pitch.your_fit: Strong — her sunscreen corpus is three posts deep inside seven days, and the US-filter explainer is 1.0d old.
angles: ‘sun water’ is not sunscreen — read the label
what counts as SPF and what just sounds like it
